title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: curveR ecosystem contains three packages share common data contract defined curveRcore. curveRcore provides data structures, preprocessing pipeline (preprocess_standards()), settings constructors (new_antigen_constraints(), new_study_params(), new_fit_options()) used identically fitting engines. curveRbayes (package) fits Bayesian hierarchical dose–response models via Stan produces posterior concentration estimates full uncertainty propagation. curveRfreq provides frequentist (NLS/NLME) alternatives settings speed regulatory precedent primary concern; head--head comparison engines ecosystem comparison vignette.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: bead_assay_example ships curveRbayes contains simulated multi-plate bead-based immunoassay data two antigens (alpha, beta), measured three plates — six curve_id values total. key design principle curve_id sole foreign key uniquely identifying one calibration curve — encoding antigen, study, experiment, plate together. curve_id_lookup decodes human-readable labels, fitting, filtering, indexing uses integer curve_id alone. raw $standards data frame carries dilution mfi. Neither concentration column log-transformed responses exist yet — created preprocessing.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: preprocess_standards() applies four steps fixed canonical order, identically curveRbayes curveRfreq: Concentration computation — (1 / dilution) × std_curve_conc, written concentration column, optionally log10-transformed. Prozone correction — compresses post-peak hook-effect deflections. Blank handling — one five strategies; "ignored" default. Response log-transform — log10(mfi), non-positive values floored adaptively transformation. preprocessing, concentration holds log10(AU/mL) mfi holds log10(MFI) — exact values Stan receives, confirmed build_stan_data() pulls standards$concentration standards[[response_variable]] directly. {r plot-preprocessed,     fig.cap="Preprocessed calibration standards antigen alpha (curve_id 1–3). axes log10-transformed — exact values passed Stan. curve_id one plate; inter-plate differences MFI level curve shape variability hierarchical model handles."} standards_preprocessed |>   dplyr::filter(curve_id %% 1:3) |>   ggplot(aes(x = concentration, y = mfi,              colour = factor(curve_id), group = curve_id)) +   geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x      = "log\u2081\u2080(concentration)  [AU/mL]",     y      = "log\u2081\u2080(MFI)",     colour = "curve_id",     title  = "Preprocessed standards — antigen alpha"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") three curves share biological dose–response differ absolute MFI offset inflection point position — precisely inter-plate variability hierarchical model designed handle.
code: # CONC in bead_assay_example runs 0.001–30 AU/mL; dilution = 1/CONC, # so std_curve_conc = 1/min(dilution) = max(CONC) = 30. antigen_settings <- new_antigen_constraints(   antigen        = "alpha",   std_curve_conc = 30,   l_asy_method   = "default",   pcov_threshold = 20 )  study_params <- new_study_params(   is_log_response    = TRUE,   is_log_independent = TRUE,   apply_prozone      = TRUE,   blank_option       = "ignored" ) # Preprocess all six curve_ids (both antigens, all plates) prep <- preprocess_standards(   data                 = bead_assay_example$standards,   antigen_settings     = antigen_settings,   response_variable    = bead_assay_example$response_var,   independent_variable = bead_assay_example$indep_var,   is_log_response      = study_params$is_log_response,   blank_data           = bead_assay_example$blanks,   blank_option         = study_params$blank_option,   is_log_independent   = study_params$is_log_independent,   apply_prozone        = study_params$apply_prozone )  standards_preprocessed <- prep$data  # After preprocessing: concentration = log10(AU/mL), mfi = log10(MFI) head(standards_preprocessed[, c("curve_id", "dilution",                                  "concentration", "mfi")], 4) #>   curve_id   dilution concentration      mfi #> 1        1 1000.00000   -1.52287875 2.039017 #> 2        1  333.33333   -1.04575749 2.500922 #> 3        1  100.00000   -0.52287875 3.054230 #> 4        1   33.33333   -0.04575749 3.618686

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: pooled model treats plates single experiment, conflating within-plate -plate variance producing overconfident predictions. separate-plate model fits plate independently, discarding shared biology producing underconfident estimates single plate calibrators. Bayesian hierarchical model occupies optimal middle ground: plate-level parameters exchangeable draws common population distribution, sharing information across plates still adapting plate’s data.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: model families share hierarchical skeleton. Using 4PL logistic (logistic4) reference: yi,k=Ak+Dk−Ak1+(xiCk)Bk+εi,k y_{,k} \;=\; A_k + \frac{D_k - A_k}{1 + \left(\dfrac{x_i}{C_k}\right)^{B_k}} \;+\; \varepsilon_{,k} kk indexes curve_id, xix_i log10-concentration standard ii (concentration column preprocessing), εi,k\varepsilon_{,k} observation noise. logistic5 loglogistic5 add per-curve_id asymmetry parameter GkG_k population mean SD. gompertz4 replaces logistic link Gompertz function keeps four-parameter hierarchy. log–log scale (is_log_independent = TRUE, is_log_response = TRUE), loglogistic4 mathematically equivalent logistic4. fit_calibration_bayes() therefore automatically drops loglogistic4 candidate set transformations active.

what: The Stan models shipped with curveRbayes
title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: Five Stan model files live inst/stan/. implements three-part structure: Paths resolved stan_model_path() models compiled (cached) per session compile_stan_model().

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: centred parameterisation θk∼𝒩(μ,σ)\theta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma) creates funnel geometry: σ\sigma small sampler must simultaneously explore tight neck wide wings, producing divergent transitions. curveRbayes uses non-centred parameterisation (NCP) throughout: θk=μθ+σθ⋅zk,zk∼𝒩(0,1) \theta_k \;=\; \mu_\theta + \sigma_\theta \cdot z_k, \qquad z_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) sampler explores standardised offset zkz_k — always flat 𝒩(0,1)\mathcal{N}(0,1) geometry — global hyperparameters (μθ,σθ)(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta) independently. likelihood evaluation recombined θk\theta_k. transformation eliminates funnel primary reason Stan achieves near-zero divergences models. parameters transformed parameters blocks logistic4 illustrate pattern: Note b d exponentiated remain strictly positive. inflection point c_par lower asymptote left fitting scale (already log10 preprocessing).
code: parameters {   // population hyperparameters (on log scale for positive quantities)   real mu_log_b;   real mu_c;          // inflection on the fitting scale   real mu_log_d;   real mu_a;          // lower asymptote (fitting scale)    // population SDs — Half-Normal keeps these positive   real<lower=0> sigma_a;   real<lower=0> sigma_log_b;   real<lower=0> sigma_c;   real<lower=0> sigma_log_d;    // non-centred offsets: one per curve_id   vector[N_plates] z_a;   vector[N_plates] z_b;   vector[N_plates] z_c;   vector[N_plates] z_d;    // observation noise (pooled across all curve_ids)   real<lower=0> sigma_obs;   real<lower=1> nu; }  transformed parameters {   // plate-level parameters reconstructed from NCP offsets   vector[N_plates] a     = mu_a      + sigma_a      * z_a;   vector[N_plates] b     = exp(mu_log_b + sigma_log_b * z_b);   vector[N_plates] c_par = mu_c      + sigma_c      * z_c;   vector[N_plates] d     = exp(mu_log_d + sigma_log_d * z_d); }

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: likelihood loop parallelised via Stan’s reduce_sum map-reduce construct, controlled grainsize argument build_stan_data() (default 1L, lets Stan choose grain automatically). CmdStan’s threading backend provides near-linear speedup number CPU cores large datasets.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: fixed_a = NULL (default), curveRbayes constructs weakly-informative priors anchored observed data range via compute_dynamic_priors(). build_stan_data() assembles named scalar fields Stan data list — prior_a_mu, prior_a_sigma, prior_d_mu, prior_d_sigma, prior_log_b_mu, prior_log_b_sigma, either prior_c_mu / prior_c_sigma (families except loglogistic4) prior_log_c_mu / prior_log_c_sigma (loglogistic4). Five-parameter families additionally receive prior_log_g_sd prior_log_g_plate_sd. Data-adaptive prior structure logistic4 / gompertz4. y_min, y_max, y_range, x_mid, x_range computed preprocessed standards. can inspect actual prior values passed Stan:

text: fitting, fit_calibration_bayes() computes Pareto-smoothed importance-sampling LOO-CV (PSIS-LOO) every fitted model via loo package (Vehtari, Gelman, Gabry 2017). LOO-CV estimates expected log predictive density (ELPD) held-observation without refitting: elpd̂loo=∑=1Nlogp(yi∣y−) \widehat{\text{elpd}}_\text{loo} \;=\; \sum_{=1}^{N} \log p(y_i \mid y_{-}) Pareto-kk diagnostic flags observations whose importance weights unreliable: {r pareto-k,     fig.cap="Pareto-k diagnostic logistic4. Points k = 0.7 (dashed line) flag high-leverage calibrators disproportionately influence fit."} (!.null(loo_list[["logistic4"]])) {   plot(loo_list[["logistic4"]],        diagnostic   = "k",        label_points = TRUE,        main         = "Pareto-k — logistic4")   abline(h = 0.7, lty = 2, col = "firebrick") }

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: Rather selecting single best model, curveRbayes computes Bayesian stacking weights (Yao et al. 2018) — weight vector 𝐰\mathbf{w} maximises stacked ELPD: 𝐰̂=argmax𝐰∑=1Nlog∑k=1Kwkp(yi∣y−,ℳk),wk≥0,∑kwk=1 \hat{\mathbf{w}} \;=\; \arg\max_{\mathbf{w}} \sum_{=1}^{N} \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \, p(y_i \mid y_{-}, \mathcal{M}_k), \quad w_k \ge 0,\; \textstyle\sum_k w_k = 1 Stacking preferable winner-takes-model selection calibrated model misspecification: even single candidate true data-generating process, stacked ensemble achieves best achievable predictive accuracy candidate set. Bayesian stacking weights across candidate models. {r stacking-bar,     fig.cap="Stacking weights. single dominant weight (> 0.9) supports using model alone; mixed weights suggest genuine model uncertainty."} (!.null(stacking_weights)) {   tibble::enframe(stacking_weights, name = "model", value = "weight") |>     ggplot(aes(x = reorder(model, -weight), y = weight, fill = model)) +     geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1),                        labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +     labs(x = "Model", y = "Stacking weight",          title = "Bayesian stacking weights") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) }
code: if (!is.null(stacking_weights)) {   tibble::enframe(stacking_weights, name = "model", value = "weight") |>     dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(weight)) |>     knitr::kable(digits = 4,                  caption = "Bayesian stacking weights across candidate models.") }

what: What is a CDAN precision grid?
title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: CDAN (Concentration-Dependent Assay Noise) precision profiling (O’Malley Deely 2003; O’Malley 2008) characterises accurately calibration curve can back-calculate unknown concentration function concentration falls curve. Near inflection point curve steep — small response uncertainty maps small concentration uncertainty. Near asymptotes curve flat — response uncertainty maps large concentration uncertainty. CDAN grid makes relationship explicit: dense grid concentration values, posterior predictive back-calculation distribution associated %CV, incorporating curve-parameter uncertainty additional measurement noise real instrument observation contribute.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: Columns CDAN grid data frame.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: ```{r grid-plot, fig.cap=“Per-model CDAN precision profiles: back-calculation %CV versus log10(concentration) curve_ids. dashed line marks 20% CV — typical regulatory acceptability threshold. ribbon spans range across curve_ids; line mean.”} all_grids <- purrr::map_dfr( names(ensemble), function(m) { g <- ensemble[[m]]$grid (.null(g)) return(NULL) dplyr::mutate(g, model = m) } ) (nrow(all_grids) > 0 && “x_fit” %% names(all_grids)) { all_grids |> dplyr::group_by(model, x_fit) |> dplyr::summarise( cv_mean = mean(pcov, na.rm = TRUE), cv_lo = min(pcov, na.rm = TRUE), cv_hi = max(pcov, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = “drop” ) |> ggplot(aes(x = x_fit, colour = model, fill = model)) + geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = cv_lo, ymax = cv_hi), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) + geom_line(aes(y = cv_mean), linewidth = 0.8) + geom_hline(yintercept = 20, linetype = “dashed”, colour = “grey40”, linewidth = 0.6) + scale_x_continuous( name = “log081080(concentration) [AU/mL]”, breaks = seq(-3, 2, = 1) ) + scale_y_continuous(name = “Back-calculation CV (%)”, limits = c(0, NA)) + facet_wrap(~model, nrow = 2) + theme_minimal(base_size = 11) + theme(legend.position = “none”) + labs(title = “CDAN precision profiles — models”, subtitle = “Ribbon = range across curve_ids; line = mean”) }
code:
dplyr::glimpse(cr1$grid) #> Rows: 200 #> Columns: 12 #> $ log10_concentration     <dbl> -4.000000, -3.972477, -3.944954, -3.917430, -3… #> $ concentration           <dbl> 0.0001000000, 0.0001065426, 0.0001135132, 0.00… #> $ x_fit                   <dbl> -4.000000, -3.972477, -3.944954, -3.917430, -3… #> $ predicted_response      <dbl> 1.391426, 1.393164, 1.394949, 1.396783, 1.3986… #> $ ci_lower                <dbl> 1.232767, 1.235067, 1.237425, 1.239843, 1.2423… #> $ ci_upper                <dbl> 1.708375, 1.708640, 1.708917, 1.709205, 1.7095… #> $ predicted_concentration <dbl> -3.922594, -3.802136, -3.814621, -3.805984, -3… #> $ se_concentration        <dbl> 0.7825477, 0.8010372, 0.7374550, 0.7870443, 0.… #> $ pcov                    <dbl> 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.00… #> $ pcov_rmse               <dbl> 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.00… #> $ pcov_pass               <lgl> FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALS… #> $ d2y_dx2                 <dbl> NA, 0.06179685, 0.06350562, 0.06526292, 0.0670… ---  # The CDAN three-step noise injection procedure {#cdan-procedure}  `predict_grid_bayes()` constructs the CDAN precision profile at each grid concentration $x^*$ via a three-step procedure that propagates both curve-parameter uncertainty and instrument measurement noise.  ## Step 1 — draw posterior curve parameters {#step1}  For each of the $S$ posterior samples, retrieve the full set of per-`curve_id` parameters:  $$  oldsymbol{	heta}_k^{(s)} = \left\{A_k^{(s)},\; B_k^{(s)},\; C_k^{(s)},\; D_k^{(s)}ight\} $$  This captures **parameter uncertainty**: how much the curve shape varies across the posterior.  ## Step 2 — evaluate the forward model {#step2}  For each sample $s$ and `curve_id` $k$, evaluate the forward model at $x^*$ (log10-concentration):  $$ 	ilde{y}^{(s,k)} = A_k^{(s)} +  rac{D_k^{(s)} - A_k^{(s)}}        {1 + \left(x^* / C_k^{(s)}ight)^{B_k^{(s)}}} $$  (For `gompertz4` the Gompertz link replaces the logistic fraction; for `logistic5`/`loglogistic5` the asymmetry parameter $G_k^{(s)}$ modifies the denominator.)  ## Step 3 — inject Student-t noise and invert {#step3}  **This step is unique to `predict_grid_bayes()` and defines CDAN.**  To each forward-predicted response $	ilde{y}^{(s,k)}$, add a noise draw from the posterior predictive distribution:  $$ y^{*(s,k)} = 	ilde{y}^{(s,k)}   + \varepsilon^{(s)}, \qquad \varepsilon^{(s)} \sim t_{
u^{(s)}}\!\left(0,\; \sigma_\varepsilon^{(s)}ight) $$  where $\sigma_\varepsilon^{(s)}$ and $
u^{(s)}$ are themselves drawn from the posterior — both `sigma_obs` and `nu` are estimated parameters in the Stan model. The heavy tails of the Student-t distribution provide a realistic characterisation of occasional outlier instrument readings.  The noisy response $y^{*(s,k)}$ is then **back-calculated** through the analytical inverse of the forward model:  $$ \hat{x}^{(s,k)} = f^{-1}\!\left(y^{*(s,k)};\;  oldsymbol{	heta}_k^{(s)}ight) $$  The collection $\left\{\hat{x}^{(s,k)}ight\}$ is the CDAN back-calculation distribution at $x^*$. The `pcov` column is the SD of this collection, divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage (computed on the fitting scale and converted to the linear CV via $	ext{pcov} = 	ext{SE} 	imes \ln(10) 	imes 100$). The `pcov_rmse` column is the relative RMSE against the known true grid point — the O'Malley (2008) CDAN precision metric.  The entire three-step logic can be illustrated concisely:   ``` r set.seed(123)  # Extract 400 posterior samples for logistic4 draws_mat <- posterior::as_draws_matrix(cmdstan_fit_4pl$draws()) idx       <- sample(nrow(draws_mat), 400) samp      <- draws_mat[idx, ]  # Plate-level parameters for curve_id = 1 A         <- as.numeric(samp[, "a[1]"]) B         <- as.numeric(samp[, "b[1]"]) C         <- as.numeric(samp[, "c_par[1]"]) D         <- as.numeric(samp[, "d[1]"]) sigma_obs <- as.numeric(samp[, "sigma_obs"]) nu        <- as.numeric(samp[, "nu"])  # Grid point: log10(1 AU/mL) = 0 x_star <- 0  # Step 1–2: forward prediction using curveRcore::logistic4() y_tilde <- curveRcore::logistic4(x_star, A, B, C, D)  # Step 3: inject Student-t noise (posterior nu), then invert eps    <- rt(length(y_tilde), df = median(nu)) * sigma_obs y_star <- y_tilde + eps  # Back-calculate using curveRcore analytical inverse x_hat <- vapply(seq_along(A), function(s) {   tryCatch(     curveRcore::inv_logistic4(y_star[s], A[s], B[s], C[s], D[s]),     error = function(e) NA_real_   ) }, numeric(1)) x_hat <- x_hat[is.finite(x_hat)]  cat(sprintf(   "CDAN back-calculation at log10(x*) = %.1f  [x* = %.0f AU/mL]
",   x_star, 10^x_star )) #> CDAN back-calculation at log10(x*) = 0.0  [x* = 1 AU/mL] cat(sprintf("  Posterior mean:  %.4f  log10(AU/mL)
", mean(x_hat, na.rm = TRUE))) #>   Posterior mean:  -0.0005  log10(AU/mL) cat(sprintf("  Posterior SD:    %.4f  log10(AU/mL)
",   sd(x_hat, na.rm = TRUE))) #>   Posterior SD:    0.0797  log10(AU/mL) cat(sprintf("  Back-calc CV:    %.1f%%
",             sd(x_hat, na.rm = TRUE) * log(10) * 100)) #>   Back-calc CV:    18.4%

title: curveRbayes
code: library(curveRbayes)  data(bead_assay_example, package = "curveRbayes")  # Preprocess standards (upstream of fitting) std_pre <- curveRcore::preprocess_standards(   data                 = bead_assay_example$standards,   antigen_settings     = antigen_settings,   response_variable    = bead_assay_example$response_var,   independent_variable = bead_assay_example$indep_var,   is_log_response      = TRUE,   is_log_independent   = TRUE )  # Fit all curves simultaneously mp <- fit_calibration_bayes(   standards          = std_pre$data,   samples            = bead_assay_example$samples,   response_var       = "mfi",   model_names        = c("logistic4", "gompertz4"),   std_curve_conc     = 30,   chains             = 4L,   warmup             = 1000L,   sampling           = 1000L,   seed               = 42 )  # One-row-per-curve summary summary_table_bayes(mp)  # All sample back-calculations collect_samples_bayes(mp)

title: curveRbayes
text: curveRbayes ships five pre-written Stan models inst/stan/. use non-centred parameterisation (NCP) eliminate Neal’s funnel geometry reduce_sum map-reduce likelihood multi-core speedup. is_log_independent = TRUE is_log_response = TRUE, loglogistic4 mathematically equivalent logistic4 automatically dropped candidate set. See vignette("bayesian-quickstart", package = "curveRbayes") full worked example including Stan model internals, MCMC diagnostics, LOO-CV model selection, CDAN precision profiling procedure.

title: curveRbayes 0.2.0
text: Initial release. fit_calibration_bayes() — hierarchical Bayesian calibration across one curve_id values simultaneously via Stan. Returns calibration_result_multiplate matching curveRfreq output contract. Five Stan model families: logistic4, logistic5, loglogistic4, loglogistic5, gompertz4 — non-centred parameterisation (NCP) reduce_sum map-reduce likelihood multi-core speedup. compile_stan_model() — compiles (caches) Stan model via cmdstanr. fit_bayes_single() — runs HMC/NUTS sampling one model family across curve_ids returns posterior draws plus NUTS diagnostics (divergences, max treedepth, E-BFMI). extract_curve_params() — extracts per-curve posterior summaries (mean, SD, 2.5/50/97.5 quantiles) fitted model object. build_stan_data() — converts preprocessed stacked standards named-list format expected Stan data {} block, including data-adaptive prior scalars. compute_dynamic_priors() — constructs weakly informative, data-adaptive hyperpriors preprocessed standards. Supports fixed_a soft constraint poorly-identified lower asymptotes. predict_grid_bayes() — CDAN (Concentration-Dependent Assay Noise) precision grid: three-step procedure posterior draw → forward evaluation → Student-t noise injection → analytical back-calculation, producing pcov pcov_rmse precision profiles every grid point. predict_samples_bayes() — back-calculates test-sample concentrations posterior draws without noise injection (observed response already noisy measurement). Returns final_concentration, se_concentration, pcov, pcov_pass per sample. compute_loo() / compare_models_loo() — PSIS-LOO cross-validation Bayesian stacking weights via loo package. summary_table_bayes() / collect_samples_bayes() — tidy extraction calibration_result_multiplate objects (multiplate legacy single-curve formats supported). Two eligibility gates active Bayesian path: rel_se (posterior SD / |mean| per parameter) dynamic_range (log10 upper/lower asymptote ratio). at_bound vcov_condition gates bypassed (hard constraints; vcov matrix). Global eligibility: model must pass gates curve_ids eligible back-calculation. bead_assay_example synthetic dataset: two antigens × three plates, six curve_id values.
